reasoning: The 30-day average of 'Strategic developments' over the past 360 days is around 20 (242 per year). A random 30-day period will typically exhibit Poisson or over-dispersed Poisson variance around this mean. To exceed the mean of 20.167, the 30-day period needs to see at least 21 events. In a typical distribution with mean ~20, an observation will exceed the mean roughly 40-45% of the time, given that the distribution of count data often displays positive skewness (meaning the median is slightly lower than the mean) or given historical bursts. Moreover, recent events in Mozambique (such as elections and the ongoing Cabo Delgado insurgency dynamics) may either stabilize or see reduced event counts compared to the preceding 360-day average which could have been elevated. Assessing a single month's total against an annual average typically results in a slightly less than 50% chance to exceed the average. Thus, a probability of around 42% is assigned.
